text: Robotic assisted partial nephrectomy for focal xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis. Partial nephrectomy is a standard treatment option for small renal masses. Partial nephrectomy has also been described for benign conditions such as xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis using an open approach. We present a case of robotic partial nephrectomy for focal xanthogranulomatous pyelonephritis. A sixty-four-year-old Caucasian female presented with left flank pain and occasional low-grade fevers, hypertension, and recurrent urinary tract infections. Creatinine clearance was sixty milliliters per minute. CT images demonstrate a seven centimeter heterogeneous left upper pole cystic lesion extending beyond the renal capsule to the perinephric space and a branching calculus in the proximal calyces consistent with focal XGP. Sagittal views are visualized.

The surgical team places a medial camera port between two robotic ports and a peri-umbilical 15mm assistant port. The colonic reflection is incised, and the bowel is mobilized medially to expose the surgical field.

The surgeon dissects the upper pole of the kidney, identifying and elevating the ureter away from the psoas muscle. Dissection progresses towards the renal hilum, where the renal hilar vessels are exposed for potential clamping. The Girotis fascia is opened at the upper pole to release the adrenal gland and expose the lesion. Dense inflammatory tissue is encountered and released, revealing the upper pole of the kidney. The remaining kidney tissue is defatted to enhance exposure.

The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to delineate the junction between normal kidney tissue and the lesion. Monopolar cautery and sharp excision are employed to excise the lesion off clamp. Distal renal artery branches are selectively dissected, identifying and clipping a segmental vessel to the kidney lesion. The anterior superior segmental artery supplying the non-viable portion of the kidney is divided.

The surgeon completes the transection and places the excised specimen into an endocatch bag. Bulldog clamps are applied to the renal vessels to enhance visualization for renorrhaphy and closure of the collecting system. The resection bed is over-sewn with 3-0 V-lock sutures, anchored with clips, and the sliding hemo-lock clip technique is used. The renal capsule is closed with interrupted sutures over hemostatic bolsters, and additional hemostatic agents are applied. The bulldog clamps are removed, and hemostasis is confirmed.

The video displays gross pathology of the excised specimen, highlighting necrotizing granulomatous inflammation associated with proximal calyceal stones. Necrotizing granulomas are visible under both low and high magnification.

Console time was one hundred eighty minutes. Warm ischemia time was fifteen minutes. Estimated blood loss was three hundred and fifty cc's. There were no intraoperative complications. The JP drain was removed on postoperative day one. patient had an uneventful post-operative course and was discharged on post-operative day two. Hemoglobin and creatinine clearance were stable at discharge and follow-up. Robotic partial nephrectomy in the setting of focal XGP is safe, feasible, and effective. This approach can facilitate a minimally invasive nephron sparing solution for these patients.
